figures.caption: The importance of taxonomy to HELM: prior benchmarks (left) test a single scenario with a narrow metric set, while HELM (right) crosses many scenarios (task/domain/language) against many metrics (accuracy, robustness, fairness, efficiency, etc.), making coverage gaps explicit.
figures.attribution: Figure from Liang et al. (2022), arXiv:2211.09110

figures.caption: Task accuracy on the same benchmark item swings from 0.036 to 0.804 across meaning-preserving prompt-format variations (separator, casing, spacing) that differ only cosmetically from the original template.
figures.attribution: Figure from Sclar et al. (2023), arXiv:2310.11324

figures.caption: SWE-bench's task construction: a real GitHub issue and codebase snapshot are given to a model, which must generate a patch; the patch is graded by running the repository's pre- and post-PR unit tests.
figures.attribution: Figure from Jimenez et al. (2023), arXiv:2310.06770

figures.caption: Distribution of estimated contamination rates across books, showing a bimodal pattern with a large cluster of books at 80-100% overlap with pretraining data, illustrating how widespread suspected contamination can be once measured directly.
figures.attribution: Figure from Shi et al. (2023), arXiv:2310.16789

figures.caption: Performance-estimation error versus number of examples used per scenario, across four major benchmark suites (Open LLM Leaderboard, MMLU, HELM, AlpacaEval): IRT-based item selection (IRT/IRT++) reaches low estimation error with far fewer examples than random sampling.
figures.attribution: Figure from Polo et al. (2024), arXiv:2402.14992

figures.caption: The same BIG-Bench tasks show no sharp emergent jump once scored with a smooth metric (negative Brier score) instead of a discontinuous one, supporting the claim that many reported emergent abilities are an artifact of metric choice rather than a genuine capability discontinuity.
figures.attribution: Figure from Schaeffer et al. (2023), arXiv:2304.15004

figures.caption: DecodingTrust's eight trustworthiness perspectives (toxicity, stereotypes, adversarial and out-of-distribution robustness, robustness to adversarial demonstrations, privacy, machine ethics, fairness), each decomposed into specific test constructions -- illustrating how a holistic safety benchmark is organized as a taxonomy rather than a single score.
figures.attribution: Figure from Wang et al. (2023), arXiv:2306.11698

figures.caption: HarmBench's framework structure: four functional categories of harmful behavior (standard, contextual, copyright, multimodal) evaluated against a matrix of attack methods and target models with optional defenses, plus a taxonomy of harm categories (bioweapon, cybercrime, misinformation, etc.).
figures.attribution: Figure from Mazeika et al. (2024), arXiv:2402.04249

figures.caption: Accuracy drops of 4.7 to 36.3 points when a biasing cue is added to the context, shown across zero-shot and few-shot prompting conditions for GPT-3.5 and Claude 1.0, while models' chain-of-thought explanations rarely mention the biasing cue as the reason for their answer.
figures.attribution: Figure from Turpin et al. (2023), arXiv:2305.04388

figures.caption: GPQA's expert-validation and non-expert-adversarial-filtering pipeline: a question passes multiple rounds of expert review and revision, then is only included in the harder Diamond subset if non-expert validators (given internet access and significant time) still answer it incorrectly.
figures.attribution: Figure from Rein et al. (2023), arXiv:2311.12022

figures.caption: Standard AlpacaEval win rates change dramatically when the same model is prompted to be concise versus verbose (e.g. GPT-4-1106-preview: 22.9 vs 64.3), while the length-controlled variant collapses this spread (41.9 vs 51.6), demonstrating a length bias in the underlying LLM-judge metric and its correction.
figures.attribution: Figure from Dubois et al. (2024), arXiv:2404.04475

figures.caption: ScienceWorld's simulated house-and-grounds environment (kitchen, workshop, greenhouse, foundry, etc.) that an agent must navigate and interact with across many rooms and objects to complete multi-step elementary-science tasks.
figures.attribution: Figure from Wang et al. (2022), arXiv:2203.07540

figures.caption: BigCodeBench's three-stage task construction: LLM-assisted data synthesis from real code, iterative human-LLM program refactoring and test-case generation, and a multi-step human curation pass (examine, pre-evaluate, cross-check) before a task enters the benchmark.
figures.attribution: Figure from Zhuo et al. (2024), arXiv:2406.15877

figures.caption: Two-axis taxonomy of the 189-paper corpus. Axis 1 (target of measurement) groups papers by what capability or property is evaluated: static knowledge/code, reasoning process depth, agentic behavior, safety/robustness properties, or the meta-evaluation process itself. Axis 2 (methodological function, applying within every Axis-1 node) separates papers that construct a new evaluation artifact from papers that audit or critique the validity of an existing one. Original figure, this survey

Evolution of LLM and agent benchmark evaluation, 2018-2026, from the GLUE-era leaderboard paradigm through saturation-driven successors, the chain-of-thought and few-shot-sensitivity reckoning, the 2023 contamination/LLM-judge/agentic-eval explosion, and the 2024-2026 critique-and-repair era. Original figure, this survey
